Is the Fetch API ES6 or a Web API? Let's Settle This

The Fetch API is one of those things a lot of people get wrong. The biggest mix-up is probably this: people can't tell whether it's part of ECMAScript 6 or part of the Web APIs. So let me break it down properly.
In an earlier post of mine,
Building Pagination in JavaScript (using the Fetch API) (article in Mandarin)
, I covered how to actually use the Fetch API. But really — is
an ECMAScript 6 thing, or is it a Web API? Let's start by looking at how MDN describes Fetch:

The Fetch API provides an interface for fetching resources (including across the network). It will seem familiar to anyone who has used XMLHttpRequest, but the new API provides a more powerful and flexible feature set. There's a global
method you can call directly, which gives you a clean, simple way to fetch resources asynchronously across the network.

From that description you can already tell one thing: Fetch came along to improve on (read: replace) the old way of doing things with XMLHttpRequest. So if you want to use the Fetch API, you've got to keep an eye on browser support — mainly because the Fetch API is built on top of the ECMAScript 6 Promise. For the full how-to, check the article I linked above,
Over on GitHub there's also a

The fetch() function is a Promise-based mechanism for programmatically making web requests in the browser. This project is a polyfill that implements a subset of the standard Fetch specification, enough to make fetch a viable replacement for most uses of XMLHttpRequest in traditional web applications.
In short, it's meant to replace the old XMLHttpRequest.
The Fetch API Is Not ES6 Syntax
After reading all those descriptions, you might already have noticed something quietly:
none of these docs say the Fetch API is ECMAScript 6 syntax
. But it's definitely part of the Web APIs. Why do I say that? First, let's look at how
— a variable-declaration keyword added in ECMAScript 6 — is categorized on MDN.
In that screenshot you can see
on MDN. So what about the Fetch API?
You can see the Fetch API is categorized under "
". So the Fetch API really isn't ECMAScript 6 syntax at all — it's an API that browsers started adding and implementing one by one around the HTML5 era. You can find the relevant background in

Fetch is the new standard that's been billed in recent years as the replacement for XHR. It's an HTML5 API, not something from the ECMAScript standard. On the browser-support side, Mozilla and Google first announced their Fetch implementations in March 2015. Currently only Firefox, Chrome, and Opera natively support it in their newer versions, and Microsoft's new Edge browser recently announced support too (probably Edge 14). Other browsers can use a polyfill as a temporary compatibility fix for now. On top of that, Fetch also relies on the ES6 Promise feature, which means if a browser doesn't have Promise, you'll need es6-promise as a polyfill as well.
Fetch isn't just a beefed-up or improved version of XHR. It's a design that approaches the problem from a different angle, even though it can do similar things. It's also built around the Promise syntax structure, and its design is low-level enough that you can configure it more flexibly to fit your actual needs. Compared to XHR's feature set, Fetch already has enough equivalent functionality to replace it — but Fetch doesn't stop there; it offers more efficient and more extensible approaches too.
So from all this you can see why the Fetch API gets mistaken for ECMAScript 6 syntax: it's because
the Fetch API has to rely on ECMAScript 6 Promise syntax
So next time someone asks you whether the Fetch API is ECMAScript 6 or a Web API, I think you'll be pretty clear on the answer.
Here are some extra notes from Huli and Chi-Wei. I'm pasting them in verbatim.

Here's a simple way to tell them apart: basically, anything you can't use directly in Node.js won't be ECMAScript — for example XHR and Fetch, or anything DOM-related.
If it were ECMAScript, then the Node.js environment should support it too. If it doesn't, then it's basically something the runtime environment provides.
Bottom line: just keep the runtime and the programming language clearly separated and you'll be fine.
And one more thing — the reverse, "anything that runs in both the browser and Node.js is ECMAScript", does NOT hold.

That's a nice simple way to tell them apart.
I also agree that "anything that runs in both the browser and Node.js is ECMAScript" doesn't hold.
Let me help out with a counterexample (?)
Take console.log — even though it works in both the browser and Node.js,
it doesn't seem to be part of the ECMAScript spec.
For the browser environment it's classified as a web API.

The Fetch API is one of those things a lot of people misunderstand. The biggest mix-up? People can't tell whether it belongs to ECMAScript 6 or to the Web APIs. So in this post let's dig into where Fetch actually comes from, why everyone assumes it's ES6, and how you can tell the two apart for good.
